what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard item that documents the risk of sync conflicts when a server-side runtime writes the same markdown ledger files that a mobile Obsidian client (via Sync or iCloud) also writes, focusing on stale overwrites and violations of an append-only history/state model.
observations: Item id item-5b224077, type block, source claude_code, channel dragnet-development, linked as part_of item-48cd6801.
The title is 'P9 · 2.3 Sync conflicts on mobile (HIGH, and it's the exact target environment' and appears truncated in the frontmatter.
It describes a user 'Tim' working from an iPhone as the target environment.
Obsidian mobile + Sync/iCloud writes whole files on reconnect with last-writer-wins or conflicted-copy semantics.
A phone reconnecting after being offline can push a stale item-xxxx.md over a newer server write.
The history/state model assumes append-only monotonic truth; sync can rewind it and produce a lying history.
iOS iCloud 'optimize storage' can evict file bodies.
Symlinks/WSL path differences may mean the runtime writes and the phone syncs are not the same filesystem location unless carefully wired.
standouts: Mobile is identified as the exact target environment and a high-risk case for concurrent writes.
Last-writer-wins sync can silently rewind append-only history/state transitions.
iCloud storage eviction and symlink/WSL path mismatch add filesystem-identity risk.
conventions: Follows the channel-memory noticeboard schema with YAML frontmatter (id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, history).
Body uses a Markdown heading and bullet list with bold emphasis for key terms.
Frontmatter title is malformed/truncated, missing the closing parenthesis.
Uses narrative framing ('Tim works from his iPhone') within a technical risk note.
concerns: Frontmatter title is truncated, which could break parsing or display.
Makes strong architectural claims without showing reproduction steps or mitigation plans in this file.
Refers to 'Tim' and 'our runtime' without defining them locally.
Assumes the reader already understands the 'two-writers problem' and the append-only ledger model.
notes: A high-priority mobile-sync risk note warning that concurrent server-side writes and phone-sync last-writer-wins can corrupt append-only ledger history.
questions: Who or what is 'Tim' in this context?
What is the specific runtime or component that writes server-side?
What mitigation, locking, or conflict-resolution strategy is planned?
How is the vault path wiring intended to be configured across WSL, Windows, and iOS?
purpose_vs_actual: Documented risk note matches its title and content: it records mobile sync-conflict risks.
apparent_themes: Distributed concurrency and the two-writers problem.
Append-only monotonic truth versus sync rewind.
Filesystem path identity across devices and sync services.
intention_signals: It appears to be flagging a critical design risk: that mobile sync semantics conflict with the ledger's append-only state model and can cause silent state reversion, urging careful path wiring and sync-aware design.
novelty: It focuses on mobile as the exact target environment and articulates how last-writer-wins can violate append-only monotonic history, while also raising iCloud storage eviction and symlink/WSL path mismatch.
contribution: Captures a concrete mobile-sync failure mode (a stale phone overwrite of server-written ledger files) and the invariant it threatens (append-only monotonic history/state), which a fused system would keep as a risk constraint.
summary_for_embedding: Noticeboard item item-5b224077 records a high-priority risk that Obsidian mobile/iCloud last-writer-wins sync can overwrite concurrently written server-side ledger files and rewind append-only history/state on iOS.
intention_for_embedding: Warn that mobile sync semantics endanger the append-only ledger truth model and require careful filesystem path alignment.
